# Materials scientist Zhenan Bao is focused on the future

October 18, 2024

* Cell Press communications team

X Facebook Reddit Tumblr Sina Weibo LinkedIn Pinterest Email

 Image credit: Przemyslaw Grygiel

Zhenan Bao, PhD, a K.K. Lee Professor of Chemical Engineering at Stanford University, was selected as one of the Cell Press 50 Scientists that Inspire for her game-changing work developing skin-inspired polymer electronics, organic field-effect transistors, and organic semiconductors.

Currently, Dr. Bao’s research aims to develop flexible and stretchable materials that are inspired by human skin. Her group integrates principles from chemistry, physics, and material sciences, and she says she is inspired by “a curiosity to know why things work the way they do and a desire to create something new.”

Prior to joining Stanford in 2004, Dr. Bao worked on the technical staff at Bell Labs, Lucent Technologies for almost nine years. She first studied chemistry at the University of Nanjing and received PhD and master’s degrees in chemistry from the University of Chicago.

Dr. Bao is an elected member of that National Academy of Sciences, National Academy of Engineering, American Academy of Arts and Sciences, and the National Academy of Inventors. In addition to her academic achievements, Dr. Bao is also the co-founder of two Silicon Valley venture funded start-ups, C3 Nano (recently acquired by Du Pont) and PyrAmes, and serves as an advising partner for Fusion Venture Capital.

In this Q&A, she reflects on what the future holds for materials science and offers words of wisdom to up-and-coming scientists.

**What are you most excited about in your field right now?**

One highlight of my career was discovering a way to make electronic skin that mimics human skin functions and even goes beyond its capabilities. The discovery of skin-like electronic materials will enable many new possibilities for understanding and improving human health. I’m excited about the potential for applying these materials to high-resolution sensors and high-speed soft integrated circuits.

**Are there barriers or hurdles you face or have had to face in your career or your science? How do you navigate these?**

I have faced many setbacks and hurdles, but I see them as part of life as well as an opportunity to re-think my goals. This allows me to look forward and focus on making things better in the future instead of dwelling on the past. For example, when an experiment does not work the way we initially thought, instead of declaring it a “failed” experiment, I encourage my students to think about whether our hypothesis may have incorrect assumptions. This frequently leads to new thinking that pushes us to develop creative new ideas that we would not have otherwise thought of.

**Do you have words of wisdom for students thinking about science—or advice you would give to your younger self?**

If you work at the intersection of what you are passionate about and what you are good at, you will always be successful. At the same time, do not forget to have compassion for yourself and those around you.

**What do the next 50 years look like for your field or for science more broadly? What changes would you like to see?**

I think the way we do research will be completely different. The boundaries between disciplines will become blurrier, and ideas will be implemented in practice much faster. I am not sure what it will be like now that AI is becoming so powerful, but I would like to see countries work together toward the betterment of mankind.

\###

As part of the celebrations for our 50th anniversary, Cell Press is honored to feature 50 notable scientists who inspire us. Editors at each of our journals across the sciences have nominated scientists whose research and careers drive innovation, cross boundaries, and inspire the leaders of the future. Visit the collection of profiles for a window into the lives and labs of these exciting scientists—and their hopes for the future of science.
